answer: A philosophical viewpoint that focuses on the benefit that the outcome of an action is bringing. If the outcome is great, the action can be morally justified no matter how cruel.
A philosophical ideal in which morality is above all else. In this argument, torture is believed to never be morally justified under any circumstance.

The text argues that the idea of trading value for morality, central to consequentialism, correlates with psychopathic tendencies, referencing Cleckley's (1941) classification of traits in individuals lacking moral sensibility.

Logistically, torture does not always bring accurate answers because the victim will say anything to make the pain stop. Physiological changes during torture can also make recollections less accurate and lead to false confessions.
An FBI interrogator who stated, 'the only thing torture guarantees you is pain,' highlighting its ineffectiveness.
A hypothetical situation where Subject A must torture innocent Subject B to prevent a nuclear bomb from detonating and killing one million innocent people, used to challenge deontological views.
A British philosopher who believed that situations of moral horror, like the 'nuclear bomb' scenario, are 'beyond morality' and even 'beyond reason,' implying they defy clear moral resolution.
